title: ML Coding Patterns
description: Six LeetCode-style but ML-focused coding problems: online K-Means, incremental AUC, LRU cache, O(1) moving average, rate limiter, and sliding window metrics — every pattern has a real ML systems application.
details:
ML coding interviews often blend LeetCode patterns with ML domain knowledge. This file covers six essential patterns: online K-Means (streaming centroid updates with k-means++), incremental AUC (Mann-Whitney U statistic for streaming evaluation), LRU cache (doubly-linked list + hashmap, used in ML model serving), O(1) moving average (SMA and EMA for monitoring), rate limiter (token bucket for model serving APIs), and sliding window metrics (precision/recall/F1 over time windows for production monitoring). Every solution is O(1) or O(log n) and production-ready.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow: Each pattern follows the same format — read the problem, understand the ML motivation, trace through the O(1) data structure, then extend it with the production variant. Start with the LeetCode-style implementation, then ask: what changes for streaming data at 100K QPS?

sections.title: Online K-Means
sections.description: [Intuition] Intuition: Standard K-Means needs all data in memory. Online K-Means processes data in streams, updating centroids incrementally with a learning rate. For non-stationary streams, a decay factor lets old centroids fade out.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict

class OnlineKMeans:
    def __init__(self, n_clusters=5, learning_rate=0.1, decay=1.0):
        self.k = n_clusters
        self.lr = learning_rate  # η: how fast centroids move
        self.decay = decay       # < 1.0 for non-stationary streams
        self.centroids = None
        self.counts = defaultdict(int)

    def _init_from_stream(self, x):
        """Reservoir sampling style k-means++ from first k points."""
        if self.centroids is None:
            self.centroids = np.zeros((self.k, len(x)))
            self.centroids[0] = x
            for i in range(1, self.k):
                # Simplified: use next k-1 distinct points
                self.centroids[i] = x + np.random.randn(len(x)) * 0.1

    def partial_fit(self, x):
        """Update centroids with a single point."""
        if self.centroids is None:
            self._init_from_stream(x)
        # Assign to nearest centroid
        dists = np.linalg.norm(self.centroids - x, axis=1)
        nearest = np.argmin(dists)
        self.counts[nearest] += 1
        # Update with learning rate (decayed by count)
        eta = self.lr / (self.counts[nearest] ** self.decay)
        self.centroids[nearest] += eta * (x - self.centroids[nearest])

    def fit_batch(self, X_batch):
        """Mini-batch update: process a batch."""
        for x in X_batch:
            self.partial_fit(x)
        return self

    def predict(self, X):
        """Assign each point to nearest centroid."""
        dists = np.linalg.norm(
            X[:, np.newaxis] - self.centroids, axis=2)
        return np.argmin(dists, axis=1)

# Usage:
# okm = OnlineKMeans(k=10, lr=0.5, decay=0.9)
# for batch in data_stream:
#     okm.fit_batch(batch)
# Usage for recommendation: stream user embeddings,
# clusters evolve with user preference drift.

# For k-means++ initialization from a reservoir:
# def kmeans_plus_plus_init(X, k):
#     centroids = [X[np.random.randint(len(X))]]
#     for _ in range(1, k):
#         dists = np.min([np.linalg.norm(X - c, axis=1) for c in centroids], axis=0)
#         probs = dists ** 2 / np.sum(dists ** 2)
#         centroids.append(X[np.random.choice(len(X), p=probs)])
#     return np.array(centroids)

sections.title: Incremental AUC Calculation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: AUC-ROC = probability that a random positive is ranked above a random negative. The Mann-Whitney U statistic computes this without sorting all predictions: AUC = U / (n_pos · n_neg) where U = Σ ranks of positives — n_pos·(n_pos+1)/2.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: For 10M predictions, sorting-based AUC costs O(n log n) ≈ 10M·23 ≈ 230M operations. Incremental AUC with running counts costs O(1) per prediction update.

sections.title: LRU Cache
sections.description: [Intuition] Intuition: LRU (Least Recently Used) cache evicts the item that hasn't been accessed for the longest time. Doubly-linked list + hashmap gives O(1) get and put. This pattern appears in ML model serving (feature cache, embedding cache) and is a favorite in ML coding rounds.
sections.codeBlock:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, key, val):
        self.key = key
        self.val = val
        self.prev = None
        self.next = None

class LRUCache:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        self.cap = capacity
        self.cache = {}  # key -> Node
        # Dummy head and tail simplify edge cases
        self.head = Node(0, 0)  # oldest (LRU)
        self.tail = Node(0, 0)  # newest (MRU)
        self.head.next = self.tail
        self.tail.prev = self.head

    def _remove(self, node):
        """Remove node from linked list."""
        prev, nxt = node.prev, node.next
        prev.next = nxt
        nxt.prev = prev

    def _add_to_tail(self, node):
        """Add node right before tail (most recent)."""
        prev = self.tail.prev
        prev.next = node
        node.prev = prev
        node.next = self.tail
        self.tail.prev = node

    def get(self, key):
        if key not in self.cache:
            return -1
        node = self.cache[key]
        self._remove(node)
        self._add_to_tail(node)  # mark as recently used
        return node.val

    def put(self, key, value):
        if key in self.cache:
            self._remove(self.cache[key])
        elif len(self.cache) >= self.cap:
            # Evict LRU = node after head
            lru = self.head.next
            self._remove(lru)
            del self.cache[lru.key]
        node = Node(key, value)
        self.cache[key] = node
        self._add_to_tail(node)

# Why LRU for ML inference?
# - Feature cache: store precomputed features for frequent users/items
# - Embedding cache: KV cache in attention, feature store lookups
# - Model registry: cache recently loaded models/adapters
# - Token cache: store frequent token embeddings
#
# interview tip: always mention the ML use case when asked to implement LRU!

sections.title: O(1) Moving Average — SMA and EMA
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Simple Moving Average (SMA) uses a circular buffer and running sum — O(1) per element with exactly W memory. Exponential Moving Average (EMA) needs one state variable and one hyperparameter α. Both are essential for model monitoring dashboards.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Monitoring latency p99 over 1-hour windows at 1000 QPS: SMA needs 3.6M values in buffer, EMA needs 1 float. This is why production monitors use EMA (or t-digest for quantiles).

sections.title: Rate Limiter (Token Bucket)
sections.description: [Intuition] Intuition: A token bucket adds tokens at rate r per second, with capacity b for bursts. Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the request is rejected. This is the most common rate-limiting algorithm for model serving APIs.
sections.codeBlock:
import time

class TokenBucket:
    def __init__(self, rate, capacity):
        """
        rate: tokens per second (refill rate)
        capacity: max burst size
        """
        self.rate = rate
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.tokens = capacity
        self.last_refill = time.monotonic()

    def _refill(self):
        now = time.monotonic()
        elapsed = now - self.last_refill
        self.tokens = min(self.capacity, self.tokens + elapsed * self.rate)
        self.last_refill = now

    def allow(self, tokens=1):
        """Return True if request is allowed."""
        self._refill()
        if self.tokens >= tokens:
            self.tokens -= tokens
            return True
        return False


class MultiTenantRateLimiter:
    """Per-tenant token buckets for multi-tenant model serving."""
    def __init__(self, default_rate, default_capacity):
        self.default_rate = default_rate
        self.default_capacity = default_capacity
        self.buckets = {}  # tenant_id -> TokenBucket

    def allow(self, tenant_id, tokens=1):
        if tenant_id not in self.buckets:
            self.buckets[tenant_id] = TokenBucket(
                self.default_rate, self.default_capacity)
        return self.buckets[tenant_id].allow(tokens)

# Production usage:
# limiter = MultiTenantRateLimiter(rate=100, capacity=200)
# if limiter.allow(user_id):
#     result = model.predict(input)
# else:
#     return 429 Too Many Requests
#
# Real ML deployments (e.g., OpenAI API):
# - Tier 1: 100 RPM, 10K TPM
# - Tier 2: 1K RPM, 100K TPM
# - Tier 3: 10K RPM, 1M TPM
# Each tier is a TokenBucket with different rate/capacity.

sections.title: Sliding Window Metrics
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Production model monitoring needs precision, recall, and F1 over a sliding time window (last 1 hour, last 1 day). A deque of (prediction, label, timestamp) with running counts gives O(1) per update and memory proportional to window size × event rate.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: For a model serving 100M predictions/day: 1-hour window = ~4.2M events. Deque with 4.2M entries × 3 fields × 8 bytes ≈ 100 MB. In practice, use EMA-based metrics instead (O(1) memory regardless of window).

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] Q1: "Design a rate limiter for a model serving API with 100K QPS." — Google L5

Token bucket: each tenant gets a bucket with rate r (tokens/second) and burst capacity b. At 100K QPS with 100 tenants, each tenant averages 1K QPS. Use per-tenant buckets with Redis for distributed enforcement. Key considerations: (1) clock synchronization across nodes — use monotonic timestamps, (2) atomic refill — use Lua scripts in Redis or local token buckets with eventual sync, (3) fallback — if Redis is down, allow requests (fail open) or reject (fail closed) based on SLA. For OpenAI-scale (1M+ tenants), use a two-tier approach: local token bucket for fast path (µs), Redis for cross-node coordination (ms).

[Interview Angle] Q2: "Implement LRU cache — why is it relevant for ML inference?" — Meta E4

Implementation: doubly-linked list + hashmap for O(1) get/put. On get: move accessed node to tail (most recent). On put: if at capacity, evict head.next (least recent), insert at tail. ML relevance: feature stores cache precomputed embeddings (e.g., user embeddings from a daily batch), model serving caches tokenized inputs, and KV cache in transformer inference is a form of LRU for long contexts. LRU's temporal locality assumption holds for ML serving because users tend to request similar items in sessions.

[Interview Angle] Q3: "How would you compute AUC-ROC from a streaming data source?" — Amazon L5

Two approaches: (1) Mann-Whitney U statistic — maintain sorted predictions with labels, O(n log n) total, computes exactly. (2) Approximate AUC using histograms — bucket predictions into 100 bins, maintain TPR and FPR per threshold, O(1) per update with ±0.001 accuracy. For production monitoring, approach (2) with EMA-decayed histograms handles distribution shift. The key insight: you don't need exact AUC for drift detection — consistent approximation is good enough.

[Interview Angle] Q4: "Design an online K-Means for a streaming recommendation system." — Meta E5

Online K-Means with mini-batch updates: (1) initialize centroids from stream using reservoir sampling (k-means++ style), (2) for each batch, assign points to nearest centroid, (3) update centroids as C_k = (1 - ρ)·C_k + ρ·(avg of assigned points) where ρ is the learning rate, (4) decay ρ over time for convergence. For non-stationary streams (user preferences drift), use a fixed small ρ or sliding window. Meta's real-time recommendation systems use a variant: hierarchical online K-Means with a first-level coarse cluster (100 centroids) and second-level fine clusters (1000 per coarse) for 10M-user retrieval.

[Interview Angle] Q5: "Your model served 100M predictions today. How do you track accuracy over the last hour?" — Google L4

Sliding window metrics with a deque of (timestamp, prediction, label). For 100M/day = ~1157 QPS, a 1-hour window holds ~4.2M events. Each event is 3×8 bytes ≈ 24 bytes → ~100 MB/host — manageable. For lower memory, use EMA-based metrics (O(1) memory). The real challenge is label feedback delay — most ML systems don't get immediate ground truth. Solution: use a buffer with TTL and only compute metrics when labels arrive (which may be hours later). For monitoring, track predicted metrics (calibration, confidence, feature distribution) in real-time and actual metrics (precision, recall) on a lagged schedule.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• LeetCode Patterns — LRU Cache (LC 146), Rate Limiter (LC 359), Sliding Window Median (LC 480)
  • Streaming Algorithms — Amit Goyal, Daum\u00e9 III & Korn (2009), online K-Means convergence proofs
  • Mann-Whitney U Test — Wilcoxon (1945), Mann & Whitney (1947): the basis for streaming AUC
  • Token Bucket — Andrew Tanenbaum, Computer Networks: the definitive token bucket reference
  • ML System Design Patterns — Google\'s TFX, Uber\'s Michelangelo monitoring architectures
